item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel bad about doing laundry now. +1

item.title: Translations
item.content:encoded:
To promote translations of my work and thank all the authors for their hard work, I am linking to them here in addition to my author page.

  • Chinese by ashausesall
  • Japanese by C-Dives
  • Portuguese by cauazinlindaum

Thank you all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I won't go the redacted URL route, but I can probably mention the platform it uses at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just puzzled about the mechanics of the blog. There should probably be a redacted URL, or a mention of the blog's presence on Blogger or some similar platform with a redacted username. I'm cool with the "how do the blog posts get to the outside world" being left undefined, but some indication of where this blog exists would help anchor the other concerns about public visibility.

item.title: Re: +101
item.content:encoded: We made it, boys.
Happy birthday to you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that it's a washing machine, of all things. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: made me smile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I understand. I did not mean to present these as individual questions, rather as support for why the Foundation should or should not make the blog inaccessible to the public; a better phrasing of my comment would be, "Couldn't and shouldn't the blog be made inaccessible to the public? If the blog was made inaccessible to the public, wouldn't it allow for more tests with notes? Wouldn't it also allow for 2620 to be reclassified as Safe?"

On a related matter, in order to minimize the likelihood of neutralization if and when the blog is made inaccessible to the public, I have come up with a somewhat more detailed plan.

+ A Somewhat More Detailed Plan
- To Clear Up the Neutralization Issue

First, the Foundation tracks down the followers of the blog (or anyone else who visited the page) via their IP addresses, and prepares to have agents or maybe even one or more MTFs ready to deploy to administer amnestics to the said individuals quietly and covertly. These agents do not deploy yet so that 2620 is not potentially neutralized.

Second, The blog is rendered inaccessible to all users other than Foundation personnel and the original followers of the blog, so as to prevent new users discovering the blog later on and making things unnecessarily complicated. Because 2620 has not lost its "fans," neutralization as a result is unlikely.

Third, Foundation personnel follow the blog from secure computers in groups of, say, five1 at a time. The groups would do this with maybe a day in between. This step begins before but happens simultaneously with the fourth step.

Fourth, the original, non-Foundation followers of the blog are administered amnestics in groups of five (see footnote), with amnestics administered to each group immediately after each group of Foundation personnel follow the blog. The computers of these people are commandeered, the accounts are made to unfollow the blog, all evidence of the blog are wiped, and the computers are made to be incapable of accessing the blog like the rest of the users on the Internet. Those who had read the blog but never followed it (meaning their computers have already been denied access in the first go around) are administered amnestics as well in a timely fashion, but the exact timing is unnecessary.

Once all of the personnel have followed the blog, all of the original followers and readers are administered amnestics, and all non-Foundation computers are denied access to the blog, testing can resume and 2620 can be reclassified as Safe.

If you choose to follow through with my idea, I would recommend simply stating in the Special Containment Procedures something along the lines of "the following/attached is a description of (Operation-Sigma-Alpha??? or some other kind of official-sounding title), which was utilized by Foundation personnel to restrict public access to SCP-2620's blog." and then making a collapsible or a link to a supplementary page that contains the above plan or a version thereof. If you would like, you could even just copy-paste what I wrote and edit it to have a proper clinical tone and appropriate conventions, tense, et cetera; just please give me some kind of credit.

If you do not want to take my advice for whatever reason, that is fine too. Just please consider it.

Footnotes
1. If they want to be really careful, the Foundation could have the personnel follow the blog one by one, and the original followers administered amnestics one by one. This might make it a bit too exhaustive and untimely, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is just too formulaic for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this is a valid concern. I've thought about this heavily while planning to write this, and during writing it. Here were the reasons why I wrote how I wrote. This also got a bit lengthy, and I'm not the best at speaking, so sorry for that in advance.

+ Sort of long essay on how I write and why.
- Sort of long essay on how I write and why.

My main goal when writing an article (be it skip or tale or author page) is to serve the narrative. This means I need to take certain liberties when writing in order to fulfill that narrative. I also try to set certain curiosities in the reader's head for them to keep reading and going along with the story. (A big inspiration for this, incidentally, is the fantastic SCP-946, by PureQuestion.)

So here's my thinking as I write this. Perhaps this blog is viewed as some novelty joke blog. It's not really popular, but it's certainly known about. We have precedent for stranger things out there. There's no real need to block people from seeing this, right? It probably only has 100, 150 followers around the time the Foundation discover this (and its history is left intentionally vague for this purpose, but this helps me with my point). So why bother blocking things off? Hell, depending on how it goes, it could probably raise more attention that not. Foundation should be resourceful.

I do this so the reader can possibly think, first off, "this SCP has a blog?", and second off, present the challenge of the Foundation containing something that's public. Presumably, the Foundation has somehow gotten control of the blog (Did they find the owner? Did they hack it? Was this set up all on its own?) and realized that you can't mess with the posts, but you can mess with other stuff. So hey, might as well present it in a way now that people keep thinking it is fake.

Here's the thing with testing with notes, then. We know that SCP-2620 can see these notes and communicate, in a sense. Does it even want to keep communicating, or would it just rather post reviews of its meals, and why does it keep finding these notes in the meals? Are the chefs in love with it? How unprofessional! You could assume the Foundation possibly tried one more time to send a note, or not, but either way, why bother? It's not something that requires stringent testing (and depending on how you view the backstory for SCP-2620, it might be useless altogether).

Euclid. I originally had a note saying that due to its autonomous posting without the prompting of loads in the washing machine, it got upgraded to Euclid. But if the blog is A) public and B) we need to monitor its posts periodically, why have it as Safe? You can't leave this alone.

Which brings me to the main thing, about making the blog inaccessible. Would this risk neutralization? No need for neutralization. Even if it didn't, how would SCP-2620 react? It can presumably sense it has followers, and can again receive and interpret notes from its "chefs", so it's not an impossible leap of logic to assume it can sense when its blog isn't being seen or not. Neutralization is a last effort once undeniable leakage of information happens (if it happens). The Foundation is perfectly content with keeping it as it is, considering they have all the information they really need (hence them ceasing testing).

- Sort of long essay on how I write and why.

This is a whole bunch of writing, but I hope I answered your questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great idea. However, doesn't the Foundation have the capabilities to make its blog inaccessible to the public? If they did that, then testing with notes could be continued and light could be shed on its origins, the nature of its abilities, how it works altogether, and so on. Also, it could be reclassified as Safe.

I am not voting for now until this is resolved, and I really like your article overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, wasn't that a charming article.

Simple, and well done. Good job Deci. +1

(Also, this post is me, critiquing an article about a critic, and I find that to be wonderfully meta.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why Euclid? Is it because it is a mostly unknown entity?

Yes. Arguably, if it didn't post on the internet, it would be safe. Hell, even when it does, it can be passed off as a novelty joke blog (which is the case in-universe, and not described in full detail in the containment procedures because that's boring to read). It's the fact that it posts on its own, without having anything loaded in it, that solidifies the Euclid class.

As far as I understand, SCP 2620 doesn't place orders, it just consumes and reviews anything edible that is thrown into it. This doesn't match up with its review.

I'm glad you caught this. It is intentional, first off. I will say Death of the Author etc. applies, so whatever you think about it is truth.

I hope you enjoyed it otherwise!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two small criticisms I have about this SCP:

1. Why Euclid? Is it because it is a mostly unknown entity?

2. The analogy presented in SCP 2620's 'critique' in experiment #42 doesn't really match up to what is happening to it. The washer says:

imagine…your server coming over to take your order

As far as I understand, SCP 2620 doesn't place orders, it just consumes and reviews anything edible that is thrown into it. This doesn't match up with its review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, maybe some sort of "What is this? Some sort of weight-loss crap?" Pin it to a skippy version of Lean Cuisine or Weight Watchers…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may explain what is happening to my socks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite sure what you're getting at, but deleting the blog or destroying the washing machine would be considered attempting to neutralize the SCP, as far as the Foundation is concerned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this. It's cute, but it's… not interesting. You've put together two disparate concepts, and you've crafted something that is two disparate concepts. It's also a great barometer of the site's newer tendency towards the slightly silly "weird thing".

Edit: Not a huge thing, but it occurs to me that no one with a knowledge of internet protocol looked at this. There's not even a mention of trying to stop the washing machine other than just finding out that they can't edit posts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic work, weird enough to entertain and leaves just enough unexplained. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah, maybe. I did consider doing something involving that, but I couldn't get it to sound funny enough, and it hurt the flow of the skip, so I cut it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is charming and funny, I love it. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I bet it's washing-machine tofu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I typically find that the more I write SCPs, the more I study ophite to learn how he makes magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of Ophite's long arm SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TOMATOOOOOOO

A good read! Blog away, washing machine.

item.title: Re: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: *poke* I suggested the number. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Depends on the type of clothing and the material made for it, but I can't imagine it'd be pleased nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then blogs about the "cuisine" it has consumed. What if one puts in it an article that is supposed to be dry-cleaned?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah yes, I know what you're referring to. That's a mind-affecting skip that's a place setting, this is a washer that criticzes food on its own, except it's not actually food?

item.title: Re: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: But Deci I looked too :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhh this one hits all the right notes for me. Sometimes I have a hard time reading decent new articles when I'm slogging through so many low effort attempts, but stuff like this makes it worth it. +1

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to Zyn, TwistedGears, and Davsto for looking it over and pointing out stuff to fix (mainly Zyn for correcting my awful english). Thanks to Fae for looking at it and liking it. Thanks to TroyL for showing interest before my internet cut out right when I was about to link him to my sandbox. Thanks to ObserverSeptember for giving me a number when I asked "What's the first number that comes into your head when you think of a washing machine, that's not 001 - 2100?".

Any grammar or spelling mistakes that are obviously wrong, please go ahead and fix. That's always my policy, this is a wiki after all. If you're not sure, ask me.

I can't think of a title for this, so don't freak out over this being [ACCESS DENIED] yet!

Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's okay. The concept is amusing, but it doesn't go anywhere besides that, although I suppose you could argue it doesn't really need to. The last addenda didn't add anything for me, but it also didn't subtract from the piece. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It vaguely reminds me of that SCP with the food review format screw, but it is unique and can carry on its own. I would like to see how it reviews a larger variety of items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tres Magnifique.
